He helped Collinsville to a 12-1 season and earned first-team All-District 5A-4 honors. This season Boomer really worked on his blocking prowess as Collinsville used a devastating rushing attack to finish as the runner-up in Class 5A. Boomer caught 15 passes for 118-yards with three touchdowns and an average of 7.9-yards a reception. His principal told us when we inquired that he didn’t have many stats (Collinsville runs the ball), but that as a player is diligent and he is consistent as he referred to Boomer’s blocking. He is a Mr. Football Oklahoma honoree from Vype Magazine. Boomer also helped the Cardinals compete in the state playoffs in 2015 and 2016 and contributed primarily as a blocker at Collinsville early in his career, hauling in 15 passes as a sophomore and two during a junior campaign with limited playing time due to injury. He was the first player in the 2019 class to commit to Oklahoma State, doing so verbally on Nov. 4, 2017.Scouting Report: He is the tight end equivalent of The Blind Side and former Ole Miss and NFL tackle Michael Oher, Boomer is big that he was a man amongst boys in Class 5A Oklahoma football. Don’t be worried that he only caught 15 passes as a senior, he could have caught 150. Boomer presents a big target and catches the ball well, but he is going to have an education ahead of him playing with athletes closer to and at his own size with more speed. I can see a transition ahead. That is not his fault, but it won’t be easy. He is going to have to learn how to use all his body and all his skills. In blocking, he plays way too high, but he could easily get away with it in high school. There is a reason he was rated so high as a prospect and it is based on his frame and athletic ability, the job now for Oklahoma State coaches and staff and Boomer is to maximize all of it.

Played for coach Gary Joseph at Katy High School and was a spectacularly impressive performer in his three seasons of high school football, amassing 4,830 rushing yards and 79 rushing touchdowns on 508 carries. Glass averaged 9.5 yards per carry throughout his Katy career, including a career-high 10.4 average as a sophomore in 2016. As a sophomore he had 184 carries for 1,912 yards and scored 30 rushing touchdowns. In that sophomore season he crossed the 100-yard mark in 10 of 13 games for Katy in 2016, including four games where he rushed for at least 245 yards and he scored multiple rushing touchdowns on nine occasions. In his junior season he rushed for 1,101 yards on 125 carries and scoring 21 rushing touchdown. As a junior he earned third-team all-state honors via the Texas Sports Writers’ Association. In his senior he rushed for 1,912 yards and 30 rushing touchdowns. He went for more than 100 yards rushing in 10 of Katy’s 13 games and he erupted for 397 yards and two touchdowns on 37 carries in Katy’s 31-28 victory over Atascocita on Sept. 8, 2018, followed up his career-high output with his third career five-touchdown game against Aguilas Blancas IPN Mexico, he also rushed for 203 yards on just 10 carries in Katy’s 63-0 shutout of Aguilas on Sept. 14. He also went over the 200-yard rushing mark in back-to-back weeks against Tompkins and Morton Ranch, rushing for 221 and 226 yards on Oct. 5 and Oct. 12, respectively. Besides being All-District again, Glass was first-team All-State by the Associated Press Sports Editors.

Lots of history to go back through here and the most recent from 2018 has Anderson helping Heritage to an 11-2 season with 72 receptions for 1,291-yards and 18 touchdowns. He rushed 14 times for 156-yards and three touchdowns. He also had four kickoff returns for a 33.8-yards average. As a junior he earned honorable mention honors on the Texas Sportswriters Association Class 4A-Division I All-State team and was first-team All-District 6-4A-I as Heritage was 12-1 and Anderson had 55 catches for 860-yards and nine touchdowns. He rushed 14 times for 143-yards and a pair of touchdowns. His sophomore season he played at Jackson Academy in Flowood, Miss. and in a 6-6 season he had 51 receptions for 765-yards and six touchdowns. He ran for 32-yard on three carries. He returned 10 kickoffs for 203-yards with a longest return of 45-yards. On defense he had eight total tackles with five unassisted, a half sack, and six passes defended.Scouting Report: I used several playoff games for this and I’m going to start out with what you give, you get or what you reap, you sew. Meaning is Anderson will fit in nicely with the Oklahoma State receiver corps because he blocks. Anderson benefits plenty from teammates blocking for him because Heritage used him a lot on quick pass and screen pass concepts. It was important to see what he was doing when he was not the target and had blocking responsibilities and he was aggressive and showed good technique, he even had a few de-cleaters putting defenders on the turf. He has strong ball skills and catches the ball well, presents a good target. He has track, straight speed, but also in the quick and screen game shows the ability to make people miss in close quarters. He is used often in jet-action sweeps or flips and has good vision, several times turning the run up, even inside the tackles when it opened early. One good example was against Life Waxahachie on a scoring run inside the 10. He is an inside receiver with outside size and skill set. Route running (especially in situations where he is not targeted) will be his avenue to work on and improve along with overall work. Not the finished product, but he shouldn’t be, and he is an excellent talent has a chance to follow in the supreme line of Cowboys receivers.

Ford had a kickoff return of 30-yards in his final game in the playoffs against Tulsa Union. How about a defensive end with a 30-yard KOR. He finished his senior season with 51 tackles, 32 unassisted, 13 tackles for loss, and 5.0 sacks. He also had 14 quarterback hurries, one fumble caused, and one recovered. On offense as a receiver he had 18 catches for 289-yards and four touchdowns. In his junior season he had 40 total tackles with 25 unassisted. He had seven tackles for loss with one fumble recovered and five fumbles caused. He also had one catch on offense for three-yards. He was an honorable mention selection to the 2017 Oklahoman All-State team. He is a Vype Oklahoma Mr. Football and a first-team All-Class 6A-1 Other accolades at Santa Fe for Ford include being a team captain, all-state honors, Defensive Player of the Year recognition and conference defensive lineman of the year in his conference. Ford also is a member of the National Honor Society and the environmental club.Scouting Report: I saw him live enough that I really didn’t have to look at too much video. He has excellent feet, good hands, and is a physical player. An example, on the plays where he gets blocked, usually because he opens up and presents too much target for the offensive lineman; he gets his satisfaction from beating on the blocker. He has an attitude on the field that somebody is going to pay. He makes a lot of plays and is able both inside and outside as Santa Fe used him some at defensive tackle as well as defensive end. He is strong enough to be effective inside (high school level) and certainly athletic enough to play on the perimeter. Loves to rush off the edge or inside but is a candidate to play “Leo” for Oklahoma State because he can drop and defend pass and did it in high school. Another candidate for special teams, maybe up back because he can block and played slot and h-back on offense. He even had that kickoff return. He will be like a block of clay for the defensive staff to mold.

Costello led San Clemente to their league title and their first since 1999. He was named MVP and earned first-team All-CIF South Coast honors for his play. The Tritons finished 7-5 as Costello completed 167-of-264 passing for 2,328-yards with 22 touchdowns and just five interceptions. He also ran for 658-yards on 108 carries and scored nine rushing touchdowns. As a junior, he completed 168-of-262 passes for 2,132-yards with 23 touchdowns and just two interceptions. In the 2017 season he ran for 392-yards and five touchdowns on 99 carries. His sophomore season he completed 18-of-37 passing attempts for 198-yards with two touchdowns and no picks. He ran for 115-yards and a touchdown on 20 carries. His career total offense is 5,823-yards and 62 touchdowns responsible for. Costello led the Tritons to 16 wins during his two seasons as the starting quarterback, including a 9-2 record in 2017 He was named first team All-South Coast in 2017 and 2018. He was named San Clemente HS Scholar Athlete of the year in 2017 and 2018 and National Football Foundation Scholar Athlete in 2018. Costello was on the Superintendent/Principal Honor Roll in 2017 and 2018Scouting Report: Let’s start with fun to watch because he makes a lot of plays and rarely is flat or at a loss to find something. He follows in the tradition of good deep throwers for Yurcich as he can throw the deep ball down the middle or on the sidelines well. He rarely misses on intermediate or deep throws short. If he misses, he is going to be long which allows receivers to know they are going to extend to or chase down the ball. On the out route he is often high, same with middle short to intermediate. His long throws are more ropes than floaters, in other words look more like Taylor Cornelius that Mason Rudolph. His release is quick, which allows him to release the ball in a slot between ¾ and over the top. The ball does drop a little low, but he brings it so quick it never mattered. He will sometimes drop between ¾ and side when throwing on the run. He has more short throws when throwing on the run, but that is the same with most quarterbacks. Once he scrambles, it is 80 percent he is going to end up running and he has good instincts. He doesn’t run away from people but avoids them and has good moves. I saw him hurdle twice and that is impressive. You can tell by the way he plays that he has some “it’. His height did not ever bother him in the three games I watched, and I watched the playoff loss where they were pounded good by St. John Bosco. He got frustrated but who wouldn’t. He was running for his skin the whole game.

Birmingham was part of an offensive line that blocked for a unit that gained 5,914-yards of total offense with 2,021-yards passing and 3,893-yards on the ground. That offense scored a total of 83 touchdowns and Katy finished 11-2 overall in the 2018 football season. Individually, the offensive line blocked for running back Deondrick Glass, who finished with 225 carries for 1,951-yards and an average of 8.7-yards per carry. He is a two-time All-District 19-6A performer that is also a second-team All-Super Team Texas Football Magazine honoree. Katy won at least 10 games in each of Birmingham’s four high school seasons, including a 16-0 state-championship run as a freshman in 2015. He finished his high school career with a 49-6 career record. Katy’s rushing attack averaged 6.9 yards per carry in 2017 and 7.4 in 2016, according to MaxPreps and Birmingham played a key role in helping Katy rack up 3,366 rushing yards and 47 touchdowns during his sophomore season in 2016Scouting Report: Very athletic for his size and moves well. His hips and core are excellent, I mean as good as you will see on an offensive tackle coming out of high school. One of my favorite aspects of offensive line play is to see the lineman tie up a defender and then initiate a twist, kind of a corkscrew move from the core that puts the defender on the turf and Birmingham can do that naturally. He has an excellent bucket step technique. He is a good blocker on the move, can shield off a defender at the first level and run and pick up a second level defender. He’s able to double-team, better as the angle than the establisher but most linemen are. Pass protection is good, arms extend and can stay locked up, but is subject to losing the quick edge rusher off the snap. Safe to say right now, while he can overpower smaller defenders he is most vulnerable to those kind of players when they move quickly off the ball and have a little shake to them. Best part about Birmingham, he rarely takes a play off. His motor runs hot and he appears to play with a mean streak.

Jernigan had a strong season as he contributed significantly on an Allen defense that held opponents to 14 points or less on eight occasions in 2018, including two shutout victories over Horn and Coppell. As a junior he was a first-team All-District 6A-9 selection and finished with 60 tackles and 14.0 tackles for loss. As a sophomore he was the District Defensive Newcomer of the Year. He was part of the Allen team that won the 2017 UIL Class 6 Division I state championship and teams that made three semifinal appearances in 2015, 2016 and 2018.Scouting Report: Jernigan plays the one technique and A-gap like a linebacker. He is not a huge overpowering guy, but he has excellent balance. He is very hard to knock off his feet or even off his stride and he can bounce a gap either way and then fight he way back into the gap he was knocked out of. That balance is what makes it really hard to nullify him. He is quick, uses his hands well, and has good vision for playing inside around a lot of people. He finds the ball quickly and attacks and makes the tackle. The biggest thing I see him needing at the college level is more strength and he will get it with the Cowboy strength and conditioning staff. He is not going to get a lot bigger but can be stronger and stay with the athletic ability and agility he has and uses to his advantage. I could see him moving around some and playing a three technique, even outside in a three-man front.

As a senior Farrar had 55 total tackles for the Raiders, who just missed upsetting DeSoto in the first round of the playoffs, losing to the powerhouse Eagles 55-53. Farrar had 39 unassisted tackles with 7.0 tackles for loss and 7.0 sacks. He also had six quarterback hurries and three fumble recoveries. His hard-hitting style of play earned him honorable mention honors at linebacker for both the 2017 Texas Sportswriters Association and the Associated Press Sports Editors All-State teams. He was also a first-team All-District 6A-9 honoree. He should repeat this season. Farrar also ran track at Skyline and had a personal best 11.33 in the 100-meter dash at the UIL 6A District 09 event in April 2018. He also posted a 19’ 10.5 personal-best long jump at the district meetScouting Report: Farrar is a big hitter, ferocious and not dirty, saw little targeting, just big hits. It is easy to see he’s very comfortable as an edge speed rusher and that is where a lot of his highlights show up. He has lots of athletic ability, but he tackles to high and doesn’t wrap up enough for me. Those are all things that are correctable. His plusses beside the ability to be very effective rushing the passer is that he has good instincts and gets to the ball quickly. When he makes mistakes, he continues playing and on occasion ends up helping finish the play. Much better against the run or as the pass rusher but show skills to improve in pass defense. He appears to be a good candidate to help on special teams early.

Wilson was on a stellar offensive line as a senior that helped Argyle deep into the playoffs and a 13-1 record overall. The offense had 3,745-yards passing and 47 touchdowns and allowed only eight sacks all season. The Eagles rushed for 2,823-yards and a 6.0-yard average on each run. That adds up to a total offense of 6,868-yards. As a junior he was first-team All-District 4-4A at offensive tackle and he was honorable mention All-State.Scouting Report: I think it would be hard not to get very excited about Wilson as in every game he does give you a flashback to Michael Oher in The Blind Side as he locks on and pushes a defender, almost always a lineman downfield some 20-30-yards even more. Wilson will be able to absorb and get better at technique like his footwork and his pass pro technique, but from the standpoint of being able to lock on to a defender and stay attached and make sure he has no chance to impact the play he is already very good. He is long arms and is very good at using them, strong hands that defenders fight but can’t get off them. If there is a physical weakness then it might be agility in his legs and feet. He can get better at moving them using his feet to gain better position off the snap of the football. I love that he has also played some defense and plays with an attitude. He can line up at tackle one play and center the next and be just as adept. Wilson has a chance to develop into a very special player. He reminds me some of Zach Crabtree coming out of high school, but better.

Miterko was a captain and the leader, playing both ways, for a hard trying 4-5-1 Carlsbad Cavemen team. Miterko earned All-District 3-6A honors in 2018 as a first-team offensive tackle and anchored an offensive line that allowed its running back to earn all-district honors and total more than 1,000 rushing yards, also allowing its fullback to average 5.2 yards per carry. He helped Carlsbad earn the No. 21 spot in MaxPreps’ 2018 New Mexico high school football rankings.Scouting Report: Almost unfair, Miterko is a down after down highlight tape because of his physical mismatch with every defensive lineman he saw in New Mexico. To top it off, he has had good coaching and shows very good technique for a high school lineman. He plays too high at times, but not as much as most high school linemen. He tries to stay low and often is still in a crouch position when he makes contact with defenders off the line of scrimmage. His technique is so good that on video it appears that he cocks his body and explodes at the point of attack when he begins the block. As a result, his targets are usually done at first contact but he stays locked on and drives or puts them in the turf. He does it time and time again. It will be fun to see him make the graduation this summer and into fall camp of playing against college defensive linemen. He is smart and very dedicated. He is going to be a leader from the time he joins the team. I wouldn’t call it a weakness, but he will get bigger and stronger and benefit from Rob Glass and his program.

Jones was having a good season, playing both ways for the Hornets when he suffered a dislocated shoulder and was done for the season. He finished with 21 total tackles and six receptions for 208-yards and two touchdowns. As a junior he had 19 total tackles with eight receptions for 135-yards and a touchdown. Jones helped guide Booker T. Washington to the Oklahoma Class 6A-II state title in 2017 and in the championship game win over Bixby, he tallied one interception, 19 tackles, eight pass receptions for 135 yards and scored a receiving touchdown. He was a team captain and all-district selection.Scouting Report: Jones is a talented athlete that has good cover schools and is willing to play more physical than most cornerbacks. He is good size and there is no doubt his basketball experience has transitioned over to help him football as he was known as an exceptional defender for the Hornets in basketball. He may be a better long ball defender than he is on the shorter stuff, but he is excellent in defending the out route. He can read and jump it. I liked his play on offense as well the past two seasons. Oddly enough, it was one of his positive traits, his desire to play physical and mix it up that may have led to the early end to his senior season and does create a question mark regarding durability. While I’ve seen him in multiple games in person, I have also seen him in practice and there is no question about his effort or his desire to play hard. He will need the weight room and that extra 20 pounds or so he will gradually put on and the muscle should help as well with his durability.

Isuman-Hundley transferred from Liberty Christian High School to Colleyville Heritage and this season he had 43 total tackles with 23 unassisted. He had two sacks for a 5-6 playoff team that was 4-3 in district play. As a junior at Liberty Christian he had 58 tackles with 11 sacks and was named TAPPS All-Division I, District 1. He was a first-team selection on the Denton Record-Chronicle’s All-Area Football Team in 2017. He originally played as a receiver, but a hand injury led to his switch to the defensive line. He also played basketball and was on the track and field team.Scouting Report: Here is a diamond in the rough for you as Isuman-Hundley has his best football ahead of him. He came out of basketball and has played just a couple of seasons of football. He is still learning the game and still learning how to use body and how to fill out his frame. He makes plays, but you can see on video that he does so almost awkwardly as he is strong, has good agility, but the instincts are still developing. The next two years he’ll benefit from working with Rob Glass and his strength and conditioning staff and then listening to Joe Bob Clements and Greg Richmond. You can see the potential, but he just needs more time to develop. He is going to be fun to watch progress because of his physical attributes.

Ross had a strong senior season with 27 tackles and a whopping 20 of those in the interior were unassisted. Then 11 of those tackles were for loss. He had 5.0 sacks and hurried the quarterback four other times. He also caused a fumble. As a junior, Ross had 45 tackles, 10 tackles for loss, three sacks, a blocked field goal, and two fumble recoveries.Scouting Report: Here is another defensive lineman that shows a lot of versatility on video as Ross can play the zero, one, and three technique. Ross looks most comfortable playing right on top of the center and his size is a great advantage. He is strong but long and shows the ability to overpower much of his competition. He is so quick off the ball that a lot of times the center can only get a piece of him and that is not enough to stop him. He is not tremendously creative as much of his pass rush is bull technique, but he is effective with it. I’m impressed with his tackling as he shows good technique. He tackles midsection to low most of the time but will use an occasional bear hug to pull down a quarterback or running back. Added strength and some extra schooling on the use of his hands will make him even more effective.

Dizadare served as the top defensive playmaker on a District 1-5A champion team that made it to the state semifinals. His highlight tape is awesome. It is tough getting statistics out of Evangel Christian, as tough as any school I have dealt with. Watching the video, you could say his number one skill is pain, kind of like Clubber Lang in the Rocky movies. He helped lead the Eagles to an 8-4 record in 2018, including a 6-0 mark in district action and guided Evangel Christian to a No. 9 ranking in the state of Louisiana as a senior, according to MaxPreps. Dizadare anchored a defensive unit that allowed 14 points or less on seven occasions in 2018 and was part of 37 victories during his high school career, including 12 during ECA’s run to the 2016 LHSAA Football Select Prep Classic state championship.Scouting Report: Dizadare is a defensive big play specialist as when he sees and dissects the play quickly and correctly is has a burst to get there and finishes. He is faster straight ahead but quick enough side-to-side to set himself up. He has good change of direction. He does tackle too high and did slide of some, but he also hit high and the slide right down to the midsection and secured the tackle. He will need to work at being more consistent. He makes a number of plays 5-10-yards from the line of scrimmage that you’d prefer be detected and addressed earlier. Another big positive is his ability in open field. He makes plays on kickoffs and punts and is very effective at limiting run opportunities in the open field. That makes him a quick candidate to see if he can help on special teams. His athletic ability is what makes him a promising player with development.

A two-time captain, Michalski helped lead St. Thomas Aquinas to a Kansas State Championship as they beat Wichita Northwest for the title. He blocked for an offense that rushed for 4,584-yards and an average of 8.0-yards on each carry. The offense also threw for 1,167-yards for a total offense of 5,751-yards. He was just awarded by the Kansas Football Coaches Association the honor of All-Senior Kansas Top 11 Player.  He is also on the Wichita Eagle Top 11 players in Kansas and a first-team selection on the Topeka Capitol Journal All-State team. Michalski earned his second straight team award as the Outstanding Offensive Lineman. He is also a two-time all-league player and back-to-back KPreps 5A offensive line all-state player. Sports in Kansas has put Michalski as all-non-senior the last two years. Michalski was Top 11 all classes in 2017.  He guided St. Thomas Aquinas to 23 wins over his final two seasons, including an unbeaten 12-0 title-winning season in 2018, and also helped the Saints finish as the Kansas Class 5A runner-up in 2017. Michalski also played baseball and participated in track and field. He was an honor roll student who completed 30 hours of volunteer service every year in high school.Scouting Report: Top quality is persistence as he never quits. If he misses his assignment, he does not quit and sulk, but finds someone else to block. You see him on some plays miss and then keep chasing the defender until he can block him. He is strong in the upper body and that is what he uses for most of his success. Like most all high school linemen, he plays too high and will need to get his pad level lower, but he moves his feet and I like him as a guard because of his ability to block on the run and pull. He has a good quick step to pull and then when he runs upfield he more resembles a back turning upfield and he clears out the hole well. In straight ahead situations and pass pro he can polish up technique as sometimes it is rough. However, he gets the job done on physical ability and that persistence. At the college level he will need technique and Josh Henson is great with teaching that. He’s a winner coming off good high school seasons every year and the state championship this past fall.

Harper had a sensational senior season as after he pledged to Oklahoma State in the summer and was named all-district, all-region and team MVP as he caught 80 passes for 1,279-yards with 18 touchdown receptions. He also rushed for 43-yards on 10 carries. On defense he had 92 total tackles, 67 unassisted stops, and an interception that he returned 95-yards for a touchdown. He also caused two fumbles and blocked a field goal. On special teams he averaged 20.6-yards on kickoff returns and had one punt return for 14-yards. His junior season he caught 69 passes for 1.086-yards and 11 touchdowns. On defense he had 40 tackles with 31 of those being unassisted. He picked off two passes and averaged 18.7-yards on kickoff returns and had a 32-yard punt return. All of that was enough to earn him a spot on the 2017 Tennessee High School Coaches Class 5A All-State team as an athlete. Playing varsity as a sophomore at Karns, Harper had 20 receptions for 310-yards and a touchdown. On defense he had 23 tackles, 16 of those unassisted and a fumble recovery. His big splash that season came on special teams where he averaged 24.3-yards on kickoff returns and had two that he returned for touchdowns. Harper finished his high school career with 155 total tackles, 114 of which were solo stops, 16 passes defended and three interceptions. He is on the honor roll and part of Mu Alpha Theta.Scouting Report: I’m going to reserve the right to re-visit Harper after he arrives in Stillwater and has to do the things he does on the field to college players, but against the Class 5A competition in Tennessee he is a freak. I saw him at Oklahoma State camp in the summer and he ran fast and had great agility. It is more impressive to see him on the football field doing football things. That word freak is used for the wide receivers at Oklahoma State and Tim Duffie may end up in a tug-of-war with Kasey Dunn for this guy. If I were to have a criticism it would be that he doesn’t touch the ball enough. He is electric on offense and he shows excellent ball skills, the ability to find space, to use his vision to escape defenders in close quarters, and the explosive speed to finish the play. I could go on writing as I saw him block effectively and carry out decoy duties. On defense he is a smart player that uses good angles in pursuit, tackles with good fundamentals, and on his one interception (he wasn’t thrown at very often) he played off the receiver and virtually deceived the quarterback into making the throw and then, like he does on offense, escaped to open space and went the distance. Harper may be one of the best prospects in this class and I find it amazing to see his offer list.

Walterscheid played both ways as a senior and on defense we found seven tackles with five unassisted stops. He had two tackles for loss and they were both quarterback sacks. He was responsible for a safety on one of those. He also played offensive tackle. Walterscheid has started for both a football state champion and a basketball state champion while at Muenster. The Hornets won the Class 21 Division II State Championship in AT&T Stadium in Arlington over Tenaha, 27-20, in 2017. Then in basketball they won a state title in 2016 and finished as semifinalists in 2017. Muenster finished 12-2 this past season. Last season Walterscheid earned All-State honors on both sides of the ball. He was first-team on offensive line for the Texas Sportswriters Association Class 2A All-State team and he was honorable mention on the defensive side for the Associated Press Sports Editors Class 2A All-State team. He was first team All-District 8-2AII on both offensive and defensive line. He is the younger brother of Oklahoma State senior defensive end and Barry Sanders Award winner Cole Walterscheid. What is interesting is that Cole was about 6-5, 195 pounds when he came to Oklahoma State and Kody is significantly larger. As mentioned above, Walterscheid has been a three-year starter in both football and basketball.Scouting Report: There was no video (full game or highlight) available from Muenster but based on what I saw of Walterscheid at camp, he is athletic for his size and being a three-sport athlete he has good agility. His father was a good athlete and with his brother having played at Oklahoma State some good knowledge has already been passed down. I would expect for him to progress, both physically and mentally, faster than Cole. As already mentioned, he comes in a lot bigger than his older brother did as a freshman.

A carpenter by trade, Hutton played for Yallourn/Yallourn North in the Mid Gippsland (Australian) Football League. He scored 27 goals in 2017 and 40 goals in the 2015 season. He is represented by ProKick Australia, a group that promotes Australian athletes for duty in both college and the NFL in the United States. According to ProKick Australia, Hutton is a more explosive punter that former Texas and now Seattle Seahawks punter Michael Dickson. He was quoted by the LaTrobe Valley Express newspaper on following his American football dream. “I was told about [American football] a few years ago so I thought 'I'll probably be too old for it now', thinking that it was like Aussie Rules and that if you were over 21 then you had no chance," he said. "But then I saw a few older blokes getting scholarships so I thought I may as well give it a go."Scouting Report: This is one of the more interesting scouting reports that I have participated in. I have seen video of Hutton punting and he looks good both with a two-step punt and a rugby style. The ball goes a long way and has good hang time, but it is an edited video tape. I’ve seen others like it and found out that the subject punted the ball 50 times to get three or four good ones. I have to take the wait and see approach on this. He is older, athletic, and looks the part.

At Abilene Christian2023 (REDSHIRT SENIOR): Played in 11 games, going 14-for-15 on field goals and adding 33 extra points for 75 total points … Averaged 62.6 yards per kickoff over 57 kicks … Recorded one tackle … Went a perfect 7-for-7 on attempts from 40-49 yards and 4-for-5 on attempts of 50+ yards, including a season-best 55-yard field goal vs. Prairie View (Sept. 9).At Houston2022 (REDSHIRT JUNIOR): Named to the College Sports Communicators Academic All-District Team after playing in nine games and going 5-for-6 on field goals, including 4-for-5 on attempts of 20+ yards … Was good on 38-of-39 extra-point attempts to score 53 total points … Averaged 50.7 yards over 13 kickoffs with a pair of touchbacks … Helped Cougars convert two onside kicks, including at Memphis (Oct. 7) that led to game-winning score … Booted three field goals at Tulsa (Nov. 26), including a season-best 42-yarder … Three field goals made matched most by a Cougar in a single game since 2016.2021 (REDSHIRT SOPHOMORE): Appeared in four games going 3-for-3 on PATs and averaging 64.2 yards per kickoff with a pair of touchbacks … Named 2021-22 UH Athletics Male Academic Cougar of the Year … 2021 Military Bowl 3M STEM Scholar-Athlete honoree.2020 (SOPHOMORE): Played in two games, going 8-for-8 on PATs vs. USF (Nov. 14) and booting a 65-yard kickoff for a touchback at Cincinnati (Nov. 7).2019 (FRESHMAN): Played in 12 games as a kickoff specialist … Averaged 57.8 yards per kickoff over 55 kicks with 25 touchbacks … Logged three tackles.HIGH SCHOOL: Played at Ridgepoint HS, where he connected on all 41 extra-point attempts as a senior and made 4-of-7 field goals while averaging 33 yards per punt … Went 7-for-7 on field goals as a junior … Also played soccer.

Member of the 2019 recruiting class.2021: Honorable mention All-Sun Betl and the highest graded interior defensive in FBS, according to Pro Football Focus, which calculated that he totaled the third-most defensive stops at the position despite ranking only 234th in snaps ... Second on the team with 9.5 tackles for loss ... Had 2.5 TFL in win over Texas State and 1.5 TFL in win over Charlotte.2020: Collected 4.5 tackles for loss, including 3.5 sacks, as a backup ... Made one tackle in the bowl game vs. Wyoming.HIGH SCHOOL: Helped Brentwood Academy in Nashville, Tenn., win its fourth straight state title in 2018 ... First-team all-state (Division II-AAA) by the Tennessee Sportswriters Association ... Named to the American Family Insurance ALL-USA Tennessee Football Team (second team) by USA Today ... All-county selection who was named Co-Defensive Player of the Year in his region ... Had three tackles for loss, including two sacks, in Brentwood’s win over Montgomery Bell in the state semifinals ... State champion heavyweight wrestler … Threw the shot put and discus for the track and field team.
